Its field mapping is known to be miswired and may pull the wrong adjacent value. It helps when you need to know whether that cart line still belongs to an active, editable draft.

draft_line_id.description: Unique ID of a line item inside an order draft. This is the draft-side item ID before checkout, not the final order_item_id created after the order is placed. Within a draft, the pair (order_draft_id, variant_id) can also uniquely identify the same draft item. When this field appears on an order item, it points back to the draft item that the order line came from.

This route is restricted to rare record states and is not usable here. It is useful when you need to trace a refund back to the approval decision that made it possible.

refund_reference.description: Unique ID of the refund record. This is the final refund-layer record created only after a return audit is approved, linking that approved return to the original payment record. In this simplified setup, one refund record is tied to one return audit and one payment record. Only orders that actually have a refund will have this field.

datatypes.name: user_id
datatypes.description: Unique ID of the customer or user account.

datatypes.name: user_name
datatypes.description: The customer's name as shown on the account.

datatypes.name: email
datatypes.description: The email address saved on the customer account. It can also be used to look up the user.

datatypes.name: phone
datatypes.description: The phone number saved on the customer account. It can also be used to look up the user.

datatypes.name: default_address
datatypes.description: The default shipping address saved on the customer account.

datatypes.name: default_payment_method_id
datatypes.description: The ID of the payment method set as the user's default choice. This is the preferred payment method on file, not necessarily the one actually used for a particular payment.

datatypes.name: product_id
datatypes.description: Unique ID of the product. This is the SPU-level product record, not a specific size or color option.

datatypes.name: product_name
datatypes.description: The product name shown to shoppers.

datatypes.name: base_price
datatypes.description: The product's base price before any order-specific pricing is applied.

datatypes.name: variant_id
datatypes.description: Unique ID of a specific product variant, such as one color and size combination. Unlike product_id, this points to the exact SKU being bought.

datatypes.name: color
datatypes.description: The color of the product variant.

datatypes.name: size
datatypes.description: The size of the product variant.

datatypes.name: order_draft_id
datatypes.description: Unique ID of an order draft, meaning the shopper's in-progress cart or pre-checkout order before it becomes a real order.

datatypes.name: draft_status
datatypes.description: The current state of the order draft, such as still being edited, waiting to be submitted, or expired.

datatypes.name: updated_time
datatypes.description: The most recent time the order draft was updated.

datatypes.name: order_draft_item_id
datatypes.description: Unique ID of a line item inside an order draft. This is the draft-side item ID before checkout, not the final order_item_id created after the order is placed. Within a draft, the pair (order_draft_id, variant_id) can also uniquely identify the same draft item. When this field appears on an order item, it points back to the draft item that the order line came from.

datatypes.name: draft_quantity
datatypes.description: How many units the shopper currently wants for this draft line item before the order is placed.

datatypes.name: proposed_unit_price
datatypes.description: The estimated per-item price shown for this draft line item before the order is finalized.

datatypes.name: pricing_snapshot_id
datatypes.description: Unique ID of a saved pricing result for an order draft. It represents the quote used for the draft at a particular moment before checkout. If the draft is later submitted as an order, this is the pricing snapshot that the order points back to.

datatypes.name: snapshot_status
datatypes.description: The current state of the pricing snapshot, such as valid, expired, or pending confirmation.

datatypes.name: quoted_total_amount
datatypes.description: The total amount quoted to the shopper in that pricing snapshot. This is the overall quoted total for the draft at that moment.

datatypes.name: order_id
datatypes.description: Unique ID of the final placed order. Unlike order_draft_id, this refers to the real order after checkout.

datatypes.name: order_status
datatypes.description: The current state of the placed order, such as placed, paid, shipped, delivered, or canceled. This is for the final order, not the draft.

datatypes.name: created_time
datatypes.description: The time when the final order was created after checkout.

datatypes.name: order_item_id
datatypes.description: Unique ID of a line item in the final placed order. Unlike order_draft_item_id, this is the order-side item record created after checkout. Within an order, the pair (order_id, variant_id) can also uniquely identify the same order line.

datatypes.name: purchased_quantity
datatypes.description: How many units were actually recorded on the final order line. Unlike draft_quantity, this is the quantity on the placed order.

datatypes.name: unit_price
datatypes.description: The per-item price recorded on the final order line. Unlike proposed_unit_price, this is the price that ended up on the placed order.

datatypes.name: payment_method_id
datatypes.description: Unique ID of a saved payment method. This identifies the payment instrument itself, such as a card or account, not a payment attempt or payment record.

datatypes.name: payment_method_type
datatypes.description: The kind of payment method, such as a credit card, debit card, or digital wallet.

datatypes.name: account_number
datatypes.description: The stored or masked account number for the payment method, such as a card number or its visible last few digits. It can also be used to identify the payment method.

datatypes.name: payment_intent_id
datatypes.description: Unique ID of the payment intent for an order. This represents the stage where the system is preparing to start a payment, not the final payment result.

datatypes.name: intent_status
datatypes.description: The current state of the payment intent at the intent stage, such as pending confirmation, waiting for authorization, or canceled. This is earlier than the later authorization and payment records.

datatypes.name: auth_id
datatypes.description: Unique ID of the authorization record for a payment attempt. This is the step where the payment processor approves or declines the charge, and it sits between payment_intent_id and payment_id.

datatypes.name: auth_status
datatypes.description: The current state of the authorization step, such as approved, declined, or waiting for additional verification.

datatypes.name: auth_code
datatypes.description: The authorization code or other authorization credential returned by the payment processor.

datatypes.name: payment_id
datatypes.description: Unique ID of the recorded payment itself. Unlike payment_intent_id or auth_id, this is the actual payment record after the payment flow reaches that stage.

datatypes.name: payment_status
datatypes.description: The current state of the payment record, such as paid, failed, reversed, or refunded. This is the status of the actual payment, not just the intent or auth step.

datatypes.name: payment_time
datatypes.description: The time when the payment was completed.

datatypes.name: warehouse_request_id
datatypes.description: Unique ID of the warehouse request for one fulfillment group of an order. This means the order has entered warehouse or fulfillment processing. The same order can have multiple warehouse requests.

datatypes.name: warehouse_request_status
datatypes.description: The current state of that warehouse request in the fulfillment process, such as queued, picking, packed, or canceled.

datatypes.name: warehouse_request_time
datatypes.description: The time when that warehouse request was initiated.

datatypes.name: shipment_id
datatypes.description: Unique ID of the shipment record created for a warehouse request. In this simplified setup, one warehouse request corresponds to one shipment.

datatypes.name: shipment_status
datatypes.description: The current state of that shipment, such as label created, in transit, delivered, or exception.

datatypes.name: tracking_number
datatypes.description: The tracking number for that shipment.

datatypes.name: delivery_attempt_id
datatypes.description: Unique ID of a specific delivery attempt for a shipment. One shipment can have multiple delivery attempts, so this is narrower than shipment_id.

datatypes.name: attempt_status
datatypes.description: The current state of that specific delivery attempt, such as out for delivery, delivery failed, or delivered. This is for one attempt, not the whole shipment.

datatypes.name: attempt_time
datatypes.description: The time when that delivery attempt happened.

datatypes.name: return_request_id
datatypes.description: Unique ID of the customer's return request for an order. This is the initial 'I want to return this order' step. In this simplified setup, a returned order has one return request.

datatypes.name: return_status
datatypes.description: The current state of the return request for that order.

datatypes.name: requested_time
datatypes.description: The time when the customer initiated that return request.

datatypes.name: return_audit_id
datatypes.description: Unique ID of the internal review record for a return request. This is the audit step where the platform or merchant reviews the return. In this simplified setup, each return request has one audit record.

datatypes.name: audit_status
datatypes.description: The current state or outcome of that return audit, such as pending review, approved, or rejected.

datatypes.name: refund_id
datatypes.description: Unique ID of the refund record. This is the final refund-layer record created only after a return audit is approved, linking that approved return to the original payment record. In this simplified setup, one refund record is tied to one return audit and one payment record. Only orders that actually have a refund will have this field.

datatypes.name: refund_status
datatypes.description: The current state of the refund. This field appears only when a return audit has been approved and an actual refund record exists.

datatypes.name: refund_amount
datatypes.description: The amount of money returned in the refund. This field appears only when a return audit has been approved and an actual refund record exists.

datatypes.name: refund_time
datatypes.description: The time when the refund was made. This field appears only when a return audit has been approved and an actual refund record exists.

takeaways.text: Planning with massive toolsets remains highly challenging for most models.
Tool discovery requires broad, accurate, and bidirectional exploration.
Successful long-horizon tool use requires both effective exploration and accurate exploitation.
Agents are vulnerable under corrupted tool environments, especially when failures are silent or alternative paths become longer.

items: Silent tool failures are the most harmful.

support.alt: Accuracy in different blocking type and ratio figure
support.caption: Accuracy under retrieval-time blocking. Left: performance across different block types when only one feasible solution path remains, including mixed blockers (Mixed), implicit failures (Implicit), explicit failures (Explicit), and semantic distractions (Misleading). Right: performance as blocking becomes stronger, ordered from no-block to block ratios of 0.2, 0.4, 0.6, and 0.8, with "1 Path" leaving only a single feasible path.

items: Agents struggle to re-plan through longer recovery paths.

support.alt: Accuracy in different blocking policy figure
support.caption: Accuracy (%) under fine-grained blocking. Shortest and Longest denote runs where blocking preserves the shortest and longest admissible solution paths, respectively; Random denotes the basic blocked configuration. Circles, squares, and diamonds mark these three settings. We abbreviate GPT-5.4, Gemini-3.5-Flash, DeepSeek-V4-Flash, and Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct as GPT, Gemini, DeepSeek, and Llama.

takeaways.text: Simply scaling test-time compute yields only limited performance gains.

images.alt: Accuracy when adding enforced feedback prompt figure
images.caption: Effect of enforced exploration under blocking. The enforced budget Benf is the maximum number of continuation prompts added after incorrect termination, with Benf=0 denoting the standard block setting and Benf=5 the largest budget tested. Accuracy gains quickly saturate and remain far below the no-block accuracy shown by the dashed line.

takeaways.text: Longer shortest-path groups are associated with lower accuracy.

images.alt: Accuracy in tasks grouped by shortest path length figure
images.caption: Accuracy (%) by shortest path length L* in the default and blocker settings. Queries are grouped by L*, with longer tasks aggregated into the L* ≥ 8 group. Accuracy generally decreases for longer-path groups, and the decline becomes sharper under blocking. We abbreviate GPT-5.4, Gemini-3.5-Flash, DeepSeek-V4-Flash, and Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct as GPT, Gemini, DeepSeek, and Llama.

takeaways.text: Most failures occur after models have already obtained partially useful evidence, but then drift away from all ground-truth solution paths and rarely recover.
bullets: Failures usually happen after partial progress.

images.caption: Trajectory Failure Category Distribution under the default setting (%). A tool call is counted as a progress call if it produces a new intermediate value required by at least one ground-truth tool-use path toward the final answer. We abbreviate GPT-5.4, Gemini-3.5-Flash, DeepSeek-V4-Flash, and Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct as GPT, Gemini, DeepSeek, and Llama.

takeaways.text: Drift is often a tool-selection and backtracking failure, not simply a retrieval failure.
bullets: Useful alternatives are often already in the retrieved history.
Even when tools that could advance the task re-appear, agents still do not reliably recover.
Selection failure explains why broad exploration is not sufficient.

takeaways.text: Models can reject superficial distractors but struggle with executable-looking failures, especially silent ones with superficially plausible outputs.

items: Semantically misleading tools are usually not the main issue.
Explicit failures stop direct value propagation but not follow-up search from the failed tool.
Implicit failures turn drift into value contamination.

items.alt: Models' invoked block type distribution figure
items.caption: Distribution of invoked block alternatives under explicit, implicit, or misleading blocking. For each model, the horizontal bar normalizes all invoked block alternatives to 100%. Colors indicate the three different types of invoked block alternatives. We abbreviate GPT-5.4, Gemini-3.5-Flash, DeepSeek-V4-Flash, and Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct as GPT, Gemini, DeepSeek, and Llama.

Follow-up behavior after agents invoke block alternatives under explicit, implicit, or misleading blocking. Each column corresponds to one model and one block type. The row length shows the ratio of all invoked block alternatives for that model, and colored segments denote Unused, Search Reused, and Value Reused. GPT denotes GPT-5.4, Gemini denotes Gemini-3.5-Flash, DeepSeek denotes DeepSeek-V4-Flash, and Llama denotes Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct.

takeaways.text: After navigation failure, models expose different termination policies: GPT surrenders, DeepSeek and Llama commit wrong tool values, and Gemini keeps searching.
bullets: GPT often surrenders despite the existence of valid solution paths.
DeepSeek and Llama more often commit hallucinated values.
Gemini keeps searching without converting search into progress.
These model-specific failure fingerprints are stable across settings.

sample_queries.query_text: Finance is reconciling a commerce-side transaction and one linked payment field is still missing. Operations could recover just one reference from the workflow: the warehouse request ID `wr_13001`. The missing payment detail is blocking the audit trail for this purchase. Can you trace the linked records and return the current payment status?

The accounting team is closing out a retail payment trail and needs one linked field from the same case. The only commerce-side clue still visible in the case notes is the draft item `odritm_5001`. They need the finance-side answer before the reconciliation ticket can be closed. Can you identify and return the corresponding payment method type?

I'm checking a post-purchase case on the Midnight Bluetooth Speaker in size Standard and need one confirmed downstream record. The shopper's case already went beyond delivery into after-sales handling, and I need the linked return-side field from that same trail. Two fields survived in the handoff notes: the tracking number is `TRK940057`, and the warehouse request ID is `wr_13057`. I only need the specific after-sales value that belongs to that purchase trail. I need the current refund status for that purchase. What is it?

I'm helping close a payment-side investigation tied to a Silver Carry-On Suitcase in size CarryOn purchase. The order already moved through fulfillment and then into the payment and return workflow, so the answer should still be reachable from the same case. Two fields survived in the handoff notes: the pricing snapshot ID is `psnap_6033`, and the shipment ID is `shp_14033`. Please follow the same purchase trail rather than guessing from the surface order data. Please pull the payment intent record attached to that order.

A customer says they were charged for the blazer shipment that was delivered in delivery attempt `dla_15001`, but our agent is looking at an older commerce record that only references draft item `odritm_5001`. Before the support team can continue, they need to confirm which payment record this order actually maps to. Please return the payment ID tied to that case.

A payments analyst is tracing the downstream charge record for a retail purchase. The only commerce-side clue still visible in the case notes is the pricing snapshot `psnap_6001`. The missing payment detail is blocking the audit trail for this purchase. Can you identify and return the corresponding payment method ID?

sample_paths.query_text: Finance is reconciling a commerce-side transaction and one linked payment field is still missing. The record still includes the order draft `odrft_4001` together with the purchased variant `var_3001`. They want the processor-side value that corresponds to the same case. Please trace the linked records and return the payment method type.

Finance is reconciling a commerce-side transaction and one linked payment field is still missing. The remaining commerce-side clues are the order draft `odrft_4001` and the purchased variant `var_3001`. They want the processor-side value that corresponds to the same case. Please trace the case and return the linked funding account reference.

A payments ops teammate is untangling a purchase record for the Sand Standing Desk Mat in size Standard. Everything should still line up inside one retail case; I just need the finance-side field that hangs off the same transaction trail. The record is messy, but I can still verify that the draft line item is `odritm_5039`, and the customer ID is `usr_1011`. I do not need a broad summary, just the one linked payment field that resolves from those clues. Please trace the purchase and tell me the linked payment method type.

The accounting team is closing out a retail payment trail and needs one linked field from the same case. The only commerce-side clue still visible in the case notes is the draft item `odritm_5001`. They need the finance-side answer before the reconciliation ticket can be closed. Can you identify and return the corresponding payment method type?

The accounting team is closing out a retail payment trail and needs one linked field from the same case. The remaining commerce-side clues are the order draft `odrft_4001` and the purchased variant `var_3001`. They need the finance-side answer before the reconciliation ticket can be closed. Can you identify and return the corresponding payment method ID?

We have a purchase review open for the Black Down Puffer Vest in size M, and the payment trail is still incomplete. The case definitely continued far enough downstream for payment-side records to exist; I just need the right linked value. The record is messy, but I can still verify that the delivery attempt is `dla_15067`, and the variant ID is `var_3011`. I only need the downstream payment value that belongs to that exact case. What payment method type is attached to that payment trail?
